COUNTY COUNSEL
ANTHONY ALBEBS
OIANE BARDSLEY

Ms. Jilf 
Stecher
counsel , Legal Division,

Practi-ces Cornmission

Sacramento, Cal- if orni-a 95804-0807
Dear Ms. Stecher:
Re: Request for Informa] Assistance
Your File No. I-92-84
we wish to thank you for giving us an opportunity to provide
you with additional- information in respect to your analysis of
the applj.cability of the Political Reform Act of 19'74, as
anended, (trthe Actrt) to planning and sponsor groups in San Diego
County. We wish to take j-ssue with that portion of your
conclusion where you state: 
rrft aLso appears that the members of
the planning and sponsor groups are public officials, 
who have
discfosure and disqual i fication requirements under the Act. r'
As you pointed out in your opinion, nenbers of planning or
sponsor groups would onJ.y constitute public officials if the
groups meet the criteria set forth in section 18700(a) (1) of the
Fair PoLiticaf Practice Commission (FPPC) Regulations which reads
as follohrs:
tt(a) 'Publ-ic official at any feveL of state or local
government I means every natural person who is a member,
officer, 
employee or consuftant of a state or local
government aqency.
u(1) rMenberr shall- include, but not be l-imj-ted
to, salaried or unsalaried members of boards or
commissions with decision-making authority. 
A board or
commj-ssion possesses decision-rnaking authority
!,rhenever:


Ms. stecher
-2-
June 24, 1992
" (A) It may make a final governmental
decision;
,,(B) It may compel a governmentaL decision;
or it may prevent a governmental decision either
by reason of an exclusive power to initiate the
decision or by reason of a veto which rnay not be
overriddeni or
,,(C) It makes substantive recommendations
which are, and over an extended period of tj.ne
have been, regularly approved without significant
amendment or modification by another public
official 
or grovernmentaf agency." (2 calif. 
code
of Regs. S l-8700 (a) (1) . )
Although you indicate that you do not have enough
information to conclude definitivel,y that planning and sponsor
groups meet any of the requirements of section 187oo(a) (1), you
assert that ttit seems l-ikely that they would meet the
requirements of either subdivisions (a) (1) (B) or (a) (1) (C) . . . .rl
There is nothing in San Diego Board of Supervisors Policy I-
1, a copy of which is attached hereto, which al"l-ous planning or
sponsor groups to conpel a decision or prevent a decision either
by an exclusive power to initiate or by a veto power which cannot
be overridden. Although the county nakes every effort to include
community representatives from the planning and sponsor groups in
the decision-making process, it is clear that the groups are
advisory only and that they do not have the power to compel a
particular decision or to bl-ock a particular decision. 
Even
though the groups may be active and infl-uentiaf in the planning
process, their power is limited to making advisory
recommendatj-ons . It seems cfear to us that pJ,anni.ng and sponsor
groups do not meet the criteria set forth in section
187oo(a) (1) (B) of the FPPC Regulations.
we also question whether pJ.anning and sponsor groups have
their substantive recomrnendations, over an extended period of
tirne, regularfy approved without significant anendment or
modification by another public officiaf or governnent agency.
Mr. John G. Mclean, Counsel for the FPPC Enforcement Division, ln
a l-etter dated April 24, l99lt a copy of which is attached
hereto, concl-uded that the Valley Center Conmunity Planning Group
(VCCPG) rrrecommendations have been regularly amended or
modified", and, 'TVCCPG members are not subject to the Act's
disclosure or disqual i fication provisions. 
(Section 82o48 and
Regul-ation 187OO ( a) ( 1) (C) . ) ',


Ms. stecher
-3-
J]JrLe 24, 4992
It seems to us that Mr. Mcl,eanrs conclusion that the Act
does not apply to the VccPG is in direct conflict with your
conclusion that the Act appears to apply to planning and sponsor
group members. lfle suggest that the discl-osure and
disqual i fication provisions of the Act only apply to members of
those pl-anning and sponsor groups, if any, which have their
substantive recommendations regularly approved without
significant amendment or modj,fication over an extended period of
tirne. To decide whether a partj.cular planning or sponsor group
meets this criteria, 
it is necessary to review the group's
substantive recommendations and then determine factual-ly whether
such recomnendations have been regufarly approved without
significant amendment or modificatj-on over an extended period of
ti.me. Any conclusion regarding the applicability of the Act to a
particular planning or sponsor qroup which is formulated vrithout
an examination of the relevant facts is of doubtful validity.
We al-so note that disclosure and disqual i fication
requj-rements similar to those contained in the Act have been made
applicable to ptanning and sponsor groups by Board of Supervisors
Policy I-1-. The Board has recognized that the advisory groups
play a vital- and hiqhly visj-bfe rofe in the planning process/ and
even though nembers of a given planning or sponsor group may not
be public officiaLs pursuant to the criteria set forth in section
18700(a) (1) of the FPPC Regulations, the members will be subject
to tocally imposed disclosure and disqual i fication requirernents.
Please reconsider your opinion in ligrht of the above
comrnents and in light of the contrary conclusions reached by the
counsel for the FPPC Enforcement Division.
Very truly yours,
LLOYD M. HARMON, JR., County Counsel

Dear I"1r. Hallabrin:
The 1991-92 san Diego County Grand Jury hereby asks the Fair
Political Practices Cornmj-ssion the following question:
Are the rnernbers of the Planning and Sponsor Groups of the
county of san Dj.ego "public officialstr within the rneaning
of Goverrunent code Section 87100 by virtue of their
membership in such groups?
It is noted that the Board of Supervi-sors of San Diego county
in its PoIicy I-l- and f-1A which cover the formation, purposes and
functions of the Planning and sponsor Groups has specified that
'rplanning group members are not county officiaLs. r' (page 7 of
enclosed Policy I-1)
The Grand Juryts concern is whether or not the Boardrs
specification that the rnernbers of the groups are not public
official-s is in conformity \"/ith the Cal,ifornia Government Code
cited above. Do the cited California Code sections provj.de basis
for the Jury to recornmend that the Board of Supervisors change its
Policy to specify that Plannj-ng and sponsor Groups are public
official,s?
As suggested by Dianne Stone of your offj-ce, copies of the
County of San Diego Board of Supervisors policies I-1 and I-1A are
enclosed, along with notations regarding the specific duties of the
groups that nay be especialfy pertinent to your decision process.
If you.have any questions regarding this matter, please eaI1
Mr. James Mitler or Mr. phil Franklin at lors; 236_2e7i.


Mr. scott Hallabrin
January 24, L992
Page T$ro
we would appreciate your prompt action on this question.
S incerelY ,
]-99]--92 SAN DIEGO COUNTY GRAND JURY
Environrnental Management conmittee
RBI4/JM: j nf
encs. (3)
(strrdies/em1)
Foreman


Enclosure 1
The San Diego county Board of Supervisors has established
planning and sponsor groups and mandated the purpose, functions,
and procedures for aII such groups in Board of Supervisors I
Policj-es I-1 and I-1A. The Board of Supervisors has provided that:
(1) Members of planning qroups are elected in efections
conducted at public expense (page 2 of Pol-icy I-1),
(2) Members of planning groups must file 
financial
dj.sclosure statements such as are required of pubLic
officials 
(page 4 of Policy I-l-),
(3) conduct and operation of the planning groups are
reguired to be governed by the Brown Act (page 5 of
Policy I-L);
(4) County staff is made available to carry out work of
the planning and sponsor groups (page I of Po]icy I-1);
(5) Publ-ic funds are budgeted to cover the work of the
planning and sponsor groups (page 8 of Policy I-1),
(6) Though County staff may input recornmendations, the
planning groups approve the final forn and content of
deveJ,opment plans which they subnit directly to the Board
of Supervisors (Plan Formulation, page 9 of Pol-icy I-1);
(7) Members of planning qroups are mandated to disqualify
thenselves from participating in actions on matters in
which they have conflicts of interest 
( Disqual ificat ion,
page 5 of Po1icy I-1);
(8) Pl-anning groups nembers may receive legal defense and
indemnification through the Office of County Counsel-
(page 7 of Po]icy I-l- and page 1 of Policy I-1A).
AII of the foregoing provisions of Policy I-l- are consistent
with the planning and sponsor groups operating as public agencies
and !/ith the mernbers of the groups being considered pubJ,ic
of f icial,s.
Holrever, the Board of Supervisors has incorporated a provisi-on
in Policy I-1 that is not consistent with the foregoing: that
members of planning and sponsor groups are lqE officers, ernployees,
or servants of the County of san Diego. This, in effect, exempts
them from jurisdiction of the FppA and the Fair politicaf iractiles
Comrnission (FPPC).

tt(a) 'Publ-ic official at any feveL of state or local

u(1) rMenberr shall- include, but not be l-imj-ted
to, salaried or unsalaried members of boards or
commissions with decision-making authority. 
A board or
commj-ssion possesses decision-rnaking authority

decision;
,,(B) It may compel a governmentaL decision;
or it may prevent a governmental decision either

have been, regularly approved without significant
amendment or modification by another public
official 
or grovernmentaf agency." (2 calif. 
code
of Regs. S l-8700 (a) (1) . )
Although you indicate that you do not have enough
